vision: A filesystem-based orchestrator that coordinates multiple AI coding agents in parallel with atomic state transitions, pluggable providers, and native macOS integration.

target_audience.primary: Software development teams using AI coding assistants who need to orchestrate multiple AI agents working in parallel on different tasks
secondary: DevOps engineers managing automated code generation workflows
Solo developers leveraging multiple AI assistants
AI/LLM researchers experimenting with agent coordination
Platform teams building internal developer tools

phases.name: Foundation (MVP Completion)
phases.description: Complete the MVP with essential reliability, usability, and recovery features. Focus on making bop production-ready for single-user and small team workflows.

milestones.title: Documentation & Onboarding Complete
milestones.description: New users can install bop and run their first AI agent workflow in under 10 minutes

milestones.title: Reliability Hardening Complete
milestones.description: bop automatically recovers from transient failures and rate limits without manual intervention

milestones.title: Workflow Management Complete
milestones.description: Users can model card dependencies, rollback failed merges, and manage complex multi-card workflows

phases.name: Enhancement (Visibility & Integration)
phases.description: Add team visibility, metrics, and integrations to scale bop to larger teams and CI/CD pipelines. Focus on observability and ecosystem connectivity.

milestones.title: Team Visibility Achieved
milestones.description: Teams can see real-time card status, metrics, and costs across all running agents in a web dashboard

milestones.title: Ecosystem Integration Live
milestones.description: bop integrates with issue trackers and adapter marketplace, enabling seamless workflow automation

phases.name: Scale (Multi-user & Advanced)
phases.description: Enable multi-user coordination, VM isolation by default, and advanced provider management. Focus on security, scalability, and experimentation.

milestones.title: VM-First Architecture Complete
milestones.description: All cards run in QEMU VMs by default with secure boot and TPM attestation (MICROCLAW)

milestones.title: Team Collaboration Enabled
milestones.description: Multiple users can coordinate card work with locking, conflict detection, and workload distribution

milestones.title: Provider Ecosystem Matured
milestones.description: Developers can easily create, test, share, and compare AI provider adapters

phases.name: Future (Vision & Innovation)
phases.description: Explore advanced features like real-time streaming, distributed coordination, and advanced workflow primitives. Focus on innovation and community feedback.

milestones.title: Real-time Streaming Available
milestones.description: Teams can watch card progress in real-time via SSE/WebSocket connections

milestones.title: Advanced Workflow Tools Available
milestones.description: Users have sophisticated conflict detection and distributed coordination capabilities

features.title: Comprehensive Getting-Started Guide
features.description: Create an interactive, end-to-end tutorial that takes new users from installation to their first successful AI agent workflow. Include quickstart commands, common workflows, troubleshooting, and best practices.
features.rationale: Addresses the major pain point of 'limited documentation for new users'. Many teams are blocked from adopting bop because they don't understand the filesystem state machine model or how to set up their first card. A great getting-started guide reduces time-to-first-value from hours to minutes.
acceptance_criteria: New user can install bop and run 'bop new' in under 5 minutes following the guide
Guide explains filesystem state machine (.cards/pending → running → done → merged) with diagrams
Tutorial includes at least 3 example workflows (simple task, multi-card parallel work, CI/CD integration)
Troubleshooting section covers common errors (adapter not found, rate limits, merge conflicts)
Guide is indexed by Spotlight and accessible via 'bop help tutorial'
user_stories: As a new user, I want a step-by-step tutorial so that I can run my first AI agent task without reading the entire codebase
As a team lead, I want onboarding documentation so that I can get my team started with bop in one sprint
As a solo developer, I want common workflow examples so that I can copy-paste and adapt them to my needs

features.title: Auto-Retry with Exponential Backoff
features.description: Automatically retry failed cards with intelligent exponential backoff when the failure is transient (network errors, rate limits, temporary API outages). Distinguish between retriable and permanent failures.
features.rationale: Addresses the pain point 'Lack of reliability guarantees when AI agents crash or rate-limit'. Currently, when a card fails due to a transient error (exit code 75 for rate limit, network timeout, etc.), it goes to failed/ and requires manual retry. This feature makes bop resilient to temporary failures without human intervention.
acceptance_criteria: Exit code 75 (rate limit) triggers retry with exponential backoff (1min, 2min, 4min, 8min, 16min, then fail)
Network errors (DNS, connection timeout) are automatically retried up to 3 times
Permanent failures (exit code 1 from policy check, compilation errors) go directly to failed/ without retry
Retry count and next retry time are stored in card meta.json
Dispatcher logs show retry attempts and backoff strategy
Max retry limit is configurable via DISPATCHER_MAX_RETRIES env var (default 5)
user_stories: As a developer running overnight batches, I want bop to auto-retry rate-limited cards so that I don't wake up to 20 failed cards
As a CI/CD pipeline, I want transient network failures to be retried automatically so that my builds don't fail unnecessarily
As a team using multiple AI providers, I want bop to wait and retry when providers are temporarily down

features.title: Card Dependency Management
features.description: Allow cards to declare dependencies on other cards via meta.json 'depends_on' field. The dispatcher will only run a card when all its dependencies are in merged/ or done/. Support dependency visualization and cycle detection.
features.rationale: Addresses the pain point 'Difficult to coordinate parallel AI work without conflicts' and known gap 'Card dependency graphs (blocking relationships)'. Teams need to model sequential workflows (e.g., 'implement auth' must complete before 'add auth to checkout') and prevent race conditions.
acceptance_criteria: Cards can declare 'depends_on': ['card-id-1', 'card-id-2'] in meta.json
Dispatcher checks dependencies before moving pending → running; blocked cards stay in pending/
Cycle detection: if A depends on B and B depends on A, both fail with clear error message
'bop status' shows dependency graph with blocked cards highlighted
'bop gantt' includes dependency edges between cards
If a dependency fails and moves to failed/, dependent cards also fail with 'dependency failed' reason
user_stories: As a developer, I want to model sequential AI tasks so that later cards don't run until prerequisites are complete
As a team, I want dependency visualization so that we can understand blocking relationships in our workflow
As a project manager, I want dependency validation so that circular dependencies are caught early

features.title: Rollback Command for Failed Merges
features.description: Add 'bop rollback <card-id>' command that undoes a merged card by reverting its commits and moving it back to pending/ for rework. Preserve rollback history in card meta.json.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'Rollback/undo commands for failed merges'. When a card passes acceptance criteria but breaks in production or causes regressions, teams need a quick way to undo the merge and rework the card. Currently, they must manually revert commits and recreate the card.
acceptance_criteria: 'bop rollback <card-id>' reverts commits associated with the card (from card meta.json 'commit_sha')
Rolled-back card moves from merged/ back to pending/ with updated meta.json
meta.json includes 'rollback_history' array with timestamps and reasons
Command requires confirmation prompt: 'This will revert N commits. Continue? [y/N]'
Works with both git and jj VCS engines
Failed rollback (e.g., merge conflicts) provides clear error message and manual instructions
user_stories: As a developer, I want to quickly undo a merged card that broke tests so that I can fix and re-run it
As a team lead, I want rollback history so that I can see which cards were reworked and why
As a CI/CD pipeline, I want automated rollback when production checks fail after merge

features.title: Dead Letter Queue for Permanent Failures
features.description: Add a 'poison/' state directory for cards that have exhausted all retries or hit permanent failure conditions. Provide 'bop rescue <card-id>' to manually recover cards from poison/.
features.rationale: Addresses pain point 'Lack of reliability guarantees when AI agents crash'. After auto-retry is exhausted (feature-2), permanently failed cards should be quarantined separately from retriable failures. This prevents dispatcher from continuously retrying hopeless cards and cluttering failed/.
acceptance_criteria: After max retries (default 5), card moves to .cards/poison/ instead of failed/
Permanent failures (exit code 2, policy violations) go directly to poison/ without retry
'bop status' shows poison/ count separately from failed/
'bop rescue <card-id>' moves card from poison/ → pending/ and resets retry count
poison/ cards are excluded from dispatcher processing (require manual intervention)
Quick Look extension shows poison/ cards with distinct visual style (skull icon)
user_stories: As a developer, I want permanently failed cards quarantined so that I can triage them separately from transient failures
As a team, I want to rescue poison cards after fixing root causes (bad adapter, missing API key) without recreating them
As a CI/CD pipeline, I want to clearly distinguish 'retry exhausted' from 'will retry later'

features.title: Web Dashboard with Real-Time Card Visualization
features.description: Build a web UI (React/Svelte) served by 'bop serve' that shows live card counts by state, card details, logs, and state transitions. Update via Server-Sent Events (SSE) when cards move between states.
features.rationale: Addresses pain point 'No visibility into agent state transitions and work progress' and known gap 'No web dashboard for team visibility'. While Finder + Quick Look is great for individuals, teams need a shared dashboard. Current Zellij status plugin is CLI-only.
acceptance_criteria: 'bop serve --dashboard' starts web UI on http://localhost:3030/dashboard
Dashboard shows card counts by state (pending, running, done, merged, failed, poison)
Click on a card to see meta.json, spec.md, prompt.md, logs, and output/
Real-time updates via SSE when cards change state (no polling)
Visual timeline showing card lifecycle (created → running → done → merged)
Filter/search by team, adapter, priority, date range
Authentication via BOP_SERVE_TOKEN (same as POST /cards/new)
Mobile-responsive design works on iPhone/iPad
user_stories: As a team lead, I want a shared dashboard so that I can see all running AI agents at a glance
As a remote team member, I want real-time card updates so that I know when my tasks complete
As a manager, I want historical card data so that I can review AI agent productivity over time

features.title: Metrics & Observability Endpoint
features.description: Expose Prometheus-compatible metrics endpoint (/metrics) with card state counts, adapter success/failure rates, queue depth, processing time, and error rates. Add Grafana dashboard template.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'Metrics/observability beyond logs and CLI status'. Production teams need to monitor bop health, detect anomalies (e.g., sudden spike in failed cards), and track SLOs (e.g., 95% of cards merge within 30min).
acceptance_criteria: 'bop serve' exposes GET /metrics endpoint in Prometheus format

Metrics include: bop_cards_total{state='pending|running|done|merged|failed|poison'}, bop_adapter_success_rate{adapter='claude|codex|...'}, bop_card_processing_time_seconds{quantile='0.5|0.95|0.99'}, bop_dispatcher_heartbeat_age_seconds
Grafana dashboard JSON template in docs/grafana/bop-dashboard.json
Metrics persist across dispatcher restarts (read from filesystem state)
Documentation includes Prometheus scrape config and Grafana setup instructions
Optional StatsD/Datadog integration via BOP_METRICS_BACKEND env var
user_stories: As a DevOps engineer, I want Prometheus metrics so that I can alert when card failure rate exceeds 10%
As a platform team, I want Grafana dashboards so that we can track AI agent utilization across teams
As an SRE, I want processing time metrics so that I can optimize adapter performance

features.title: Adapter Marketplace & Registry
features.description: Create a community registry of adapters (similar to Homebrew taps or NPM) where users can discover, install, and publish adapters. 'bop adapter search <query>' finds adapters, 'bop adapter install <name>' downloads from registry.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'Adapter marketplace or registry for discovering community providers'. Currently, users must manually copy .nu scripts to adapters/. A registry enables ecosystem growth, quality ratings, and version management.
acceptance_criteria: 'bop adapter search <query>' queries registry API and shows matching adapters with descriptions and ratings
'bop adapter install <name>' downloads adapter .nu script to adapters/ and validates signature
'bop adapter publish' uploads local adapter to registry (requires GitHub auth)
Registry hosted at registry.bop.dev with web UI for browsing adapters
Each adapter has metadata: name, description, author, version, dependencies, license, download count
Adapters signed with GPG/cosign for security (optional but recommended)
Version pinning: 'bop adapter install anthropic-claude@1.2.3'
Adapter testing framework validates adapter contract before publishing
user_stories: As a developer, I want to discover adapters for new AI providers so that I don't have to write them from scratch
As an adapter author, I want to publish my adapter so that the community can use it
As a security-conscious team, I want signed adapters so that I can trust registry downloads

features.title: GitHub Issues & Linear Integration
features.description: Automatically create cards from GitHub Issues or Linear tickets. 'bop sync-issues' pulls new issues and creates .card bundles. When cards merge, update issue status and link PR.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'Integration with issue trackers (GitHub Issues, Linear, Jira)' and pain point 'CI/CD integration with AI agents is complex'. Teams want to trigger AI agent work from their existing workflow tools without manual card creation.
acceptance_criteria: 'bop sync-issues' creates cards from issues labeled 'bop-auto' (configurable via BOP_ISSUE_LABEL)
Card spec.md is populated from issue body; meta.json includes issue URL and number
When card merges, GitHub/Linear issue status updates to 'Done' with link to merged PR
Supports both GitHub Issues (via gh CLI) and Linear (via LINEAR_API_KEY)
Bi-directional sync: issue comments appear in card meta.json, card logs added to issue comments
'bop new --from-issue <url>' creates single card from issue URL
Works with webhooks: GitHub/Linear webhook → bop serve → auto-create card
user_stories: As a product manager, I want to create Linear tickets that automatically become bop cards so that I don't need to learn bop CLI
As a developer, I want issue status to update when AI agent work completes so that stakeholders see progress
As a team, I want GitHub issue discussions to sync with card logs so that context is preserved

features.title: Cost Tracking Dashboard
features.description: Track and visualize AI provider API costs per card. Parse adapter logs for token counts, estimate costs using provider pricing tables, and show cumulative spend in dashboard and CLI.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'Cost tracking across AI providers'. Teams using bop at scale need to understand their AI spend, identify expensive cards, and optimize adapter selection to stay within budget.
acceptance_criteria: Adapters report token usage in card meta.json: { 'tokens_in': 1000, 'tokens_out': 500, 'provider': 'claude-3-7-sonnet' }
Cost calculator uses pricing table (docs/pricing.json) to estimate cost per card
'bop status --costs' shows total spend and per-adapter breakdown
Web dashboard shows cost trends over time (daily/weekly/monthly)
Budget alerts: log warning when daily spend exceeds BOP_COST_BUDGET env var
Export cost reports as CSV for accounting: 'bop cost-report --from 2026-01-01 --to 2026-01-31 > costs.csv'
user_stories: As a finance team, I want to track AI API costs so that I can allocate budget accurately
As a developer, I want to see which cards are expensive so that I can optimize prompts or switch adapters
As a team lead, I want cost alerts so that we don't exceed our monthly AI budget

features.title: VM-First Dispatcher Mode (MICROCLAW)
features.description: Make QEMU VM execution the default path for all cards (not just qemu.nu adapter). Each card runs in isolated VM with dub bootloader, zam unikernel, 9P filesystem mount for card bundle, and UART output capture.
features.rationale: Addresses technical debt 'Migration to VM-first architecture (MICROCLAW) in progress' and known gap 'VM isolation not yet default'. Security, reproducibility, and attestation are core to bop's long-term vision. Current host-based adapters expose risk of untrusted AI-generated code.
acceptance_criteria: Dispatcher invokes qemu-system-x86_64 for all cards by default (not 'nu adapters/claude.nu')
Card bundle mounted via 9P filesystem (-virtfs local,path=.card,mount_tag=card)
VM boots dub EFI → zam unikernel reads card/spec.md → calls AI provider → writes output/ → exits
TPM vTPM measurements verify boot chain integrity (PCR0-7)
Exit codes: 0=success, 75=rate-limit, other=failure (same as current adapters)
VM timeout enforced by QEMU watchdog (default 1 hour, configurable via card meta.json)
Fallback to host adapters via BOP_DISABLE_VM=1 env var (for development/debugging)
Documentation includes QEMU installation and zam build instructions
user_stories: As a security-conscious team, I want all AI-generated code to run in isolated VMs so that malicious output can't compromise my system
As a researcher, I want reproducible builds with TPM attestation so that I can verify card execution integrity
As a platform team, I want VM isolation by default so that untrusted users can run cards safely

features.title: Multi-User Coordination Primitives
features.description: Enable multiple dispatchers to run concurrently with distributed locking (Redis/etcd), workload stealing, and conflict resolution. Support team-based card assignment and routing.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'Multi-user coordination primitives (currently optimized for single dispatcher instance)'. Large teams need multiple dispatcher instances for high throughput and fault tolerance. Current single-instance design bottlenecks at ~100 concurrent cards.
acceptance_criteria: Multiple dispatcher processes can run concurrently with Redis/etcd distributed locks (BOP_LOCK_BACKEND=redis://localhost:6379)
Workload stealing: idle dispatcher claims pending cards from other dispatchers
Team-based routing: cards in .cards/team-X/ only processed by dispatchers with TEAM=X env var
Conflict detection: if two dispatchers grab same card, second one backs off and logs warning
Dispatcher health checks: distributed heartbeat with leader election for monitoring
Graceful shutdown: dispatcher releases locks and returns running cards to pending/
'bop dispatcher --multi-user' enables distributed mode (defaults to single-instance for backwards compatibility)
user_stories: As a large team, I want to run 5 dispatcher instances so that we can process 500 cards in parallel
As a DevOps engineer, I want distributed locking so that multiple bop instances don't conflict
As a team lead, I want team-based routing so that frontend cards only go to frontend-team dispatchers

features.title: Provider Quality A/B Testing Framework
features.description: Run the same card through multiple AI providers (Claude, Codex, Ollama) in parallel and compare results. Measure success rate, cost, speed, and code quality. Generate comparison reports.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'A/B testing framework for comparing provider quality'. Teams want data-driven decisions on which AI provider to use for different task types (e.g., Codex for TypeScript, Claude for documentation, Ollama for low-cost tasks).
acceptance_criteria: 'bop new --ab-test claude,codex,ollama' creates 3 cards with same spec but different adapters
After all variants complete, 'bop ab-report <card-prefix>' generates comparison report
Report includes: success/failure, processing time, cost, output diff, acceptance criteria pass rate
Web dashboard shows A/B test results with side-by-side code diff
Automated provider selection: BOP_AUTO_PROVIDER=best chooses highest-quality provider based on historical A/B data
'bop ab-history' shows past A/B tests with win rates per provider
user_stories: As a developer, I want to test whether Claude or Codex is better for my task so that I can optimize for quality
As a cost-conscious team, I want to compare cheap vs expensive providers so that we can balance cost and quality
As a researcher, I want historical A/B data so that I can publish papers on AI provider performance

features.title: Adapter Scaffolding CLI Tool
features.description: Generate boilerplate adapter code with 'bop adapter new <name>'. Creates .nu template with parameter handling, error logging, rate-limit detection, and contract compliance. Includes test harness.
features.rationale: Lowers the barrier to creating new adapters (currently ~20 LOC but requires understanding the calling convention). Makes it trivial to add new AI providers and encourages community contributions.
acceptance_criteria: 'bop adapter new my-provider' creates adapters/my-provider.nu with template code
Template includes: argument parsing, prompt file reading, API call skeleton, error handling, exit codes (0/75/1)
Generated adapter passes 'bop adapter test my-provider' (validates calling convention)
Template comments explain each section with links to adapter docs
Supports multiple languages: --lang=nu (default), --lang=python, --lang=bash
'bop adapter new --example' creates working example adapter (mock provider with random success/failure)
user_stories: As a developer, I want to scaffold an adapter in 30 seconds so that I can focus on the API integration logic
As a community contributor, I want a template that passes tests so that my adapter is accepted in the registry
As a team, I want adapters in Python so that our ML engineers can create providers without learning Nushell

features.title: Template Registry & Discovery
features.description: Create a library of card templates (spec.md + prompt.md patterns) for common tasks: 'Add REST endpoint', 'Fix bug', 'Write tests', 'Refactor component'. 'bop new --template <name>' instantiates from template.
features.rationale: Addresses known gap 'Template library and discovery mechanism'. Writing effective spec.md and prompt.md requires skill. Templates codify best practices and reduce time-to-first-card for new users.
acceptance_criteria: 'bop templates' lists available templates from registry (local + remote)
'bop new --template rest-endpoint' creates card from template with placeholders
Templates include: spec.md skeleton, prompt.md with best-practice wording, example acceptance criteria
Template variables: {{PROJECT_NAME}}, {{LANGUAGE}}, {{FRAMEWORK}} replaced during instantiation
Community templates published to registry (same as adapters): 'bop template publish my-template'
Templates categorized: feature, bugfix, refactor, test, docs, infra
Template ratings and download counts shown in 'bop templates'
user_stories: As a new user, I want templates so that I don't have to write spec.md from scratch
As a team, I want to share internal templates so that cards follow our standards
As a community member, I want to discover popular templates so that I can learn best practices

features.title: Card Event Stream (SSE/WebSocket)
features.description: Provide real-time event stream of card state transitions, log lines, and adapter output. Clients subscribe via WebSocket or SSE to get instant updates. Enables live dashboards and integrations.
features.rationale: Enhances the web dashboard (feature-6) and enables integrations (Slack bots, mobile apps, IDE plugins) that need real-time card updates without polling the filesystem.
acceptance_criteria: 'bop serve' exposes WebSocket endpoint at ws://localhost:3030/events
Clients subscribe to events: CARD_CREATED, CARD_RUNNING, CARD_DONE, CARD_MERGED, CARD_FAILED, LOG_LINE
Events include full card meta.json in JSON payload
Authentication via BOP_SERVE_TOKEN (same as dashboard)
SSE fallback for clients without WebSocket support: GET /events (Server-Sent Events)
Example integrations: Slack bot posts when cards merge, VS Code extension shows live card count
user_stories: As a team, I want Slack notifications when critical cards merge so that we can react immediately
As a developer, I want VS Code extension to show live card status so that I don't have to switch windows
As a mobile user, I want push notifications when my cards complete so that I can review on the go

features.title: Advanced Conflict Detection & Resolution
features.description: Detect when multiple cards modify the same files and provide merge conflict resolution UI. Show diffs, allow manual merge or auto-resolve with AI assistance.
features.rationale: Addresses pain point 'Difficult to coordinate parallel AI work without conflicts'. When two cards modify the same function, merge conflicts require manual resolution. This feature automates detection and provides guided resolution.
acceptance_criteria: Before merging, merge-gate detects conflicts with already-merged cards
Conflicting cards shown in 'bop conflicts' with file-level diff highlighting
Web dashboard includes conflict resolution UI: three-pane diff (base, card A, card B)
Auto-resolve option: 'bop resolve --auto <card-id>' uses AI to merge conflicting changes
Manual resolution workflow: 'bop resolve <card-id>' opens editor with conflict markers
Conflict history tracked in meta.json: which cards conflicted, how resolved
user_stories: As a developer, I want conflict detection before merge so that I don't break the build
As a team, I want AI-assisted conflict resolution so that we don't spend hours on manual merges
As a reviewer, I want to see which cards conflicted so that I can understand the merge strategy

features.title: Distributed Multi-Dispatcher Coordination
features.description: Scale bop to 100+ dispatcher instances with distributed consensus (Raft/Paxos), global work queue, and cross-datacenter replication. Enable cloud-scale AI agent orchestration.
features.rationale: Long-term vision for enterprises running thousands of AI agents across multiple regions. Requires distributed systems engineering but enables massive scale.
acceptance_criteria: Dispatchers form Raft consensus cluster with leader election
Global work queue distributed via consistent hashing (card ID → dispatcher)
Cross-datacenter replication: cards created in US-East replicate to EU-West
Fault tolerance: if dispatcher crashes, leader redistributes its cards to other nodes
Observability: distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) shows card flow across dispatchers
'bop cluster status' shows health of all dispatcher nodes
user_stories: As an enterprise platform team, I want 100 dispatcher instances so that we can run 10,000 cards per day
As a global team, I want multi-region replication so that developers in Europe see low latency
As an SRE, I want fault tolerance so that dispatcher crashes don't lose card state
